coredata.dc:description:
Ni-rich layered oxides are the most promising cathode materials for high-energy-density Li-ion batteries. Full utilization of their potential resulting from high nickel content, mainly high capacity, is impaired due to the rapid performance degradation. Mitigating the main limitations of Ni-rich materials is possible through element doping. In this work, a series of LiNi
0.6
Mn
0.2
Co
0.2
O2 (NMC622) cathode materials doped with sodium and/or neodymium was synthesized and systematically studied (XRD, SEM, TEM, STEM-EDX, XPS, galvanostatic charge/discharge tests, cyclic voltammetry). The strategies of single-element and dual-element doping were applied to study the effect of Na and Nd doping on the electrochemical performance of the modified NMC622 materials. The Na-doped NMC622 material exhibited improved capacity retention of 80.5% after 100 cycles, which is superior to the undoped one (62.7%), possibly owing to enlarged Li layer spacing and decreased Li ion migration activation energy. For the Nd-doped NMC622 cathode material, both the initial discharge capacity and capacity retention were much improved compared to the undoped NMC622. This can be related to the enhanced structural stability brought by the formation of strong bonds between neodymium and oxygen atoms. In contrast, the dual-doping of Na and Nd in NMC622 material resulted in much poorer electrochemical and cycling performance, but the reason for this is unclear. The experimental data suggest that the combination of Na and Nd dopants caused the deterioration of crystal structure, possibly due to the introduced impurities. This consequently affected the structural stability of the dual-doped material, leading to the lowest discharge capacity and capacity retention among the studied NMC622 materials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Ni-rich layered oxides are the most promising cathode materials for high-energy-density Li-ion batteries. Full utilization of their potential resulting from high nickel content, mainly high capacity, is impaired due to the rapid performance degradation. Mitigating the main limitations of Ni-rich materials is possible through element doping. In this work, a series of LiNi
0.6
Mn
0.2
Co
0.2
O2 (NMC622) cathode materials doped with sodium and/or neodymium was synthesized and systematically studied (XRD, SEM, TEM, STEM-EDX, XPS, galvanostatic charge/discharge tests, cyclic voltammetry). The strategies of single-element and dual-element doping were applied to study the effect of Na and Nd doping on the electrochemical performance of the modified NMC622 materials. The Na-doped NMC622 material exhibited improved capacity retention of 80.5% after 100 cycles, which is superior to the undoped one (62.7%), possibly owing to enlarged Li layer spacing and decreased Li ion migration activation energy. For the Nd-doped NMC622 cathode material, both the initial discharge capacity and capacity retention were much improved compared to the undoped NMC622. This can be related to the enhanced structural stability brought by the formation of strong bonds between neodymium and oxygen atoms. In contrast, the dual-doping of Na and Nd in NMC622 material resulted in much poorer electrochemical and cycling performance, but the reason for this is unclear. The experimental data suggest that the combination of Na and Nd dopants caused the deterioration of crystal structure, possibly due to the introduced impurities. This consequently affected the structural stability of the dual-doped material, leading to the lowest discharge capacity and capacity retention among the studied NMC622 materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are currently considered the most suitable for portable electronic devices and electric vehicles (Ding et al., 2019; Zubi et al., 2018; Armand et al., 2020; Yoo et al., 2014). Despite their high energy density, they have some disadvantages, e.g., relatively high price, loss of capacity due to ageing or sensitivity to temperature changes. However, the latest research clearly shows that the possibilities of achieving better operational parameters of lithium-ion batteries lie primarily in improving cathode materials—the primary determinant of cell properties (Nitta et al., 2015; Fergus, 2010; Schipper et al., 2017). A basic requirement for cathode materials is the ability to accept and release lithium ions quickly and reversibly. Transition metal oxides with a layered structure exhibit significant potential in this respect due to the presence of two-dimensional lithium ion diffusion channels and the stability of the layered structure during their intercalation–deintercalation process (Julien et al., 2014). Cathode materials for LIBs consist of lithiated transition metal oxides, including LiCoO2 (LCO), LiMn2O4 (LMO), LiFePO4 (LFP), as well as multi-component cobalt-containing oxides such as lithium-nickel-aluminium-cobalt oxide (NCA) and lithium-nickel-manganese-cobalt oxide (NMC) (Mohamed and Allam, 2020; Manthiram, 2020). Currently, lithiated transition metal oxides LiNi
x
Mn
y
Co
z
O2 (including NMC333, NMC532, and NMC622) obtained by partial replacement of cobalt ions by nickel and manganese ions are materials commercially used to power electric vehicles, portable power tools or electrical energy storage systems due to good efficiency, high capacity, low cost, good stability and safety. The latest literature reports and information from battery manufacturers indicate that NMC materials with a high Ni content will be the next generation of cathode materials (Li et al., 2020a; Xia et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2015). Nowadays, the greatest challenge is to optimize the procedure for their production in terms of increasing the diffusion coefficients of lithium ions and counteracting the phenomenon of Ni/Li cation mixing, which would significantly improve the functional properties of lithium-ion batteries (Zhang, 2020; Jiang et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2019).

Mitigating the main limitations of nickel-rich cathode materials is possible due to their modifications by element doping (Cui et al., 2023; Qian et al., 2022; Li et al., 2020b; Yan et al., 2020). Dopants introduced into the oxide layers are usually electrochemically inactive, have large ionic radii and form strong bonds with oxygen. In this way, they have a stabilizing effect on the structure of the cathode material. For Ni-rich materials, the positions occupied by nickel ions may be replaced by cations of Al
3 +
, B
3 +
, La
3 +
, Eu
3 +
, Nd
3 +
, Sn
4 +
, Ce
4 +
, Ti
4 +
, Zr
4 +
, Nb
5 +
, V
5 +
, Mo
6 +
(Cui et al., 2023; Weigel et al., 2019; Aurbach et al., 2015; Zybert et al., 2022; Schipper et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2021). Introducing ions with an ionic radius larger than the lithium ion radius (e.g., Na
+
, Mg
2 +
) into the lithium layers makes it possible to widen the migration path of Li
+
ions by increasing the distance between the layers and, consequently, improving the lithium diffusion kinetics during the cell operation (Xie et al., 2016; He et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). It is assumed that they act as pillars stabilizing the structure of these materials, i.e., supporting the layered structure and preventing its local collapse caused by Ni/Li cation mixing.

The single-element doping usually improves a specific property of the cathode material or has limited influence on the electrochemical performance (i.e., capacity, electrical conductivity, stability or efficiency during high-voltage cycling). The strategies of dual-site doping (Liu et al., 2019; Chu et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2016) and dual-element doping (Peng et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2021; Ghorbanzadeh et al., 2018) were introduced to promote the doping effect. For example, Chu et al. (2021) adopted a dual-site doping approach to enhance the electrochemical performance and structural stability of Ni-rich cathode materials. Nb
5 +
cations occupied both the positions of Li
+
ions in the interlayer spaces and the positions of the transition metals in the oxide layers. Dual-site doping with Nb suppresses Ni/Li cation mixing, stabilizes the layer structure, and prevents local collapse and structural distortion. Increased capacity retention, rate capability and improved lithium-ion transport were also observed. Similar results were obtained by Xu et al. for NCA cathode material when dual-site doping with Zn was applied (Xu et al., 2021). The presence of Zn
2 +
ions at transition metal sites and Li
+
ion sites promoted the formation of a typical layered structure during sintering, inhibited Li/Ni mixing and accelerated reversible Li
+
intercalation. In the case of Ti
4 +
(Wu et al., 2019) and Zr
4 +
(Wang et al., 2016), pillar effect and strong TiO and ZrO bonds enhanced interface stability and improved lithium-ions transport. Peng et al. (2019) applied the strategy of dual-element doping using Al and B as dopants. The synergistic effect of these elements greatly enhanced the electrochemical performance of the cathode material. B
3 +
ions placed into the tetrahedral site blocked the migration of transition metal ions, and Al
3 +
acted as pillars in the octahedral site, stabilizing the structure and suppressing the phase transition during cycling. Similar observations were described by Tang et al. (2021), where the Ce-B-dual-doped material showed excellent structural stability and significantly suppressed voltage decay. Cerium ions stabilized the structure and improved the diffusion kinetics of Li
+
ions, while boron ions prevented oxygen loss through a strong BO bond. In the work of Ghorbanzadeh et al. (2018), Li
+
and Mn
4 +
ions in NMC622 material were simultaneously replaced by larger ions of Na
+
and Nb
5 +
, respectively. The dual-doped material exhibited better electrochemical performance at high rates and excellent cycling stability. Na substitution enhanced the layer distance and Li ions migration. Nb substitution changed the lattice parameters and increased electron concentration, preventing the impedance increase during the cycling process.

In this work, the LiNi
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
O2 (NMC622) cathode was modified by the dual-doping using two different dopants (neodymium and sodium). It was intended to replace a part of the Ni
3 +
ions in the oxide layers with Nd
3 +
ions and a part of the Li
+
ions in the interlayer spaces with Na
+
ions. The research hypothesis was to observe the synergistic effect of two different dopants located in two different positions and playing different roles. The main goal was to obtain efficient NMC622 cathode materials through the simultaneous introduction of Na and Nd ions, which may have a positive effect on both the stabilization of the crystal structure and the improvement of the Li
+
ion diffusion process, and, consequently, significantly improve the electrochemical performance of the studied cathode material.

LiNi
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
O2 sample was prepared by impregnating co-precipitated Ni
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
CO3 with an aqueous lithium salt solution according to the procedure described in detail in our previous papers (Zybert et al., 2022, 2020). Briefly, carbonate of transition metals was obtained using Ni(NO3)2
⋅ 6H2O, Mn(NO3)2
⋅ 6H2O, and Co(NO3)2
⋅ 6H2O with a molar ratio of Ni:Mn:Co equal 6:2:2 as the precursors of transition metals. An aqueous solution of Na2CO3 and ammonia water were used as a precipitating agent and chelating agent, respectively. The obtained precipitate was impregnated with an aqueous solution of lithium acetate dihydrate (3 mol% excess of lithium), dried and calcined in the air (500 °C, 5 h and then 900 °C, 15 h). The obtained cathode material was denoted as NMC622.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Na + NMC622 material doped with sodium
ce:section.ce:para:
Li
0 . 99
Na
0 . 01
Ni
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2

0 . 2
O2 sample was prepared by co-impregnating the carbonate precursor Ni
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
CO3 with an aqueous solution of lithium acetate dihydrate (3 mol% excess of lithium) and sodium acetate using a rotary evaporator. The sodium doping amount of 1 mol% was chosen as it was determined to be the optimal Na dopant concentration according to the previous literature reports (Xie et al., 2016; He et al., 2019). Then, the powder was dried at 120 °C for 18 h and calcined in the air at 500 °C for 5 h and 900 °C for 15 h. The obtained cathode material was denoted as Na + NMC622.

Li(Ni
0 . 6
Mn

)
0 . 995

0 . 005
O2 sample was prepared by impregnating the co-precipitated (Ni
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
)
0 . 995
Nd
0 . 005
CO3 with an aqueous solution of lithium salt according to the procedure described in detail in our previous paper (Zybert et al., 2022). Briefly, carbonate of transition metals and neodymium was obtained using Ni(NO3)2
⋅ 6H2O, Mn(NO3)2
⋅ 6H2O, Co(NO3)2
⋅ 6H2O (molar ratio of Ni:Mn:Co equal 6:2:2) and Nd(NO3)3
⋅ 6H2O as the precursors of transition metals and dopant, respectively. The neodymium dopant amount of 0.5 mol% was chosen as it was determined to be the optimal Nd dopant concentration according to our previous report (Zybert et al., 2022). An aqueous solution of Na2CO3 and ammonia water were used as a precipitating agent and chelating agent, respectively. The obtained precipitate was impregnated with an aqueous solution of lithium acetate dihydrate (3 mol% excess of lithium), dried and calcined in the air (500 °C, 5 h and then 900 °C, 15 h). The obtained cathode material was denoted as NMC622 + Nd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Na + NMC622 + Nd material dual-doped with sodium and neodymium
ce:section.ce:para:
Li
0 . 99
Na
0 . 01
(Ni
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
)
0 . 995
Nd
0 . 005
O2 sample was prepared by co-impregnating the (Ni
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
)
0 . 995
Nd
0 . 005
CO3 carbonate precursor with an aqueous solution of lithium acetate dihydrate (3 mol% excess of lithium) and sodium acetate using a rotary evaporator. The dopant amounts were 1 mol% of Na and 0.5 mol% of Nd. The dopant amounts were the same as those in the single-doped cathodes. This was because these Na and Nd dopant concentrations were the optimal values in their single-doped counterparts (Zybert et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2016; He et al., 2019). Then, the powder was dried at 120 °C for 18 h and calcined in the air at 500 °C for 5 h and 900 °C for 15 h. The obtained cathode material was denoted as Na + NMC622 + Nd.
The list of the obtained cathodes and their specific surface areas is reported in Table S1.

TEM investigations were conducted using a FEI Talos F200X transmission microscope at 200 kV. The specimens were suspended in ethanol and dropped onto thin amorphous carbon embedded on a Cu grid. The morphological observations were performed in STEM modes using the high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) detector and the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) for chemical composition mapping. SEM investigations were performed using a Zeiss Crossbeam XB54 microscope with InLens detector.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using Mg K α (h
ν = 1253 . 7
eV
) radiation. A Prevac system equipped with Scienta SES 2002 electron energy analyzer operating at constant transmission energy (Ep = 50 eV) was used to acquire experimental data. The pressure in the analysis chamber was kept under 1 ⋅ 10−9 mbar. A small amount of powder sample was placed on a molybdenum sample holder and introduced into the UHV system. Charging effects were corrected, assuming that the C 1s photoelectron line has a binding energy of 285.0 eV. The reproducibility of the peak position thus obtained was ± 0.2 eV.

Prepared NMC622 cathode materials, carbon black and PVDF were mixed with a weight ratio of 8:1:1 and dispersed in N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent. The slurry was applied onto an aluminium foil (current collector) and dried at 120 °C in a vacuum oven. The electrochemical performances were measured in three-electrode Swagelok-type cells assembled in an argon-filled glove box (O
2
and H
2
O < 10 ppm). The prepared NMC622 materials were a working electrode (9 mm in diameter, working area of 0.64 cm2, electrode mass loading about 2.5 mg cm −2) and two lithium electrodes acted as counter and reference electrodes (10 mm in diameter each). 1 M LiPF6 in EC:DMC 1:1 wt./wt. was used as the electrolyte. The cells were tested at 25 °C with a battery cycler (Atlas Sollich 0961) at the rate of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 1C between 2.8 to 4.3 V vs. Li/Li
+
. During high-rate tests, the cells were cycled first at 0.1C (10 cycles), then at 0.2, 0.5, and 1C (series of 5 cycles), and at 0.1C during the last 20 cycles. The cycle life of the cells was analyzed by 100 cycles at 0.5C. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed at the 0.4, 0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 mV s−1 speed rates between the 2.8 and 4.5 V voltage range vs. Li/Li
+
. Before the scans, an open circuit potential (OCP) was measured, and the first cycle started at the OCP value and continued towards 4.5 V. The Li
+
diffusion coefficient was calculated using the Randles–Sevcik equation.

Table 1 presents the electrochemical properties of the obtained cathode materials. It is visible that pristine NMC622 exhibits the first cycle charge and discharge capacities equal to 184.4 mAh g−1 and 146.6 mAh g−1, respectively. A low Coulombic efficiency (about 79%) is observed. The addition of Na does not change the performance of the Na + NMC622 material. Whereas for the material doped with Nd (NMC622 + Nd), an increase in the first cycle discharge capacity is observed. In this case, the irreversible capacity loss is reduced, which contributes to the increased Coulombic efficiency. A more pronounced difference in the electrochemical performance is clearly visible for the dual-doped Na + NMC622 + Nd material. The first cycle discharge capacity is about 30% lower than the other studied materials. The irreversible capacity loss is substantial, resulting in a low Coulombic efficiency (about 66%). There is only a slight difference in the values of the Li
+
diffusion coefficient for the NMC622 pristine and Na-doped materials. The fastest diffusion is observed for the Nd-doped material, i.e., the one with the highest capacity among the studied materials. It was impossible for the dual-doped Na + NMC622 + Nd material to determine the Li
+
diffusion coefficient because the electrochemical process was not diffusion controlled. This presumably results from a different powder morphology, structural disorders and/or a change in the mechanism of the occurring processes. The rate performance results between 0.1 and 1C (Fig. 1a) reveal that all the studied materials exhibit high discharge capacities of 135–150 mAh g−1 at low current density (0.1C) except for the Na + NMC622 + Nd electrode. However, with the continuous increase of discharge rate (0.2, 0.5, and 1C), the Nd-doped cathode shows higher rate capacities than the undoped NMC622 cathode. Na-doped cathode material has a discharge capacity slightly lower than the NMC622 pristine. During the first 10 cycles at 0.1C, the decrease of the discharge capacity for all the electrodes is the highest because of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) formation. At this stage, the capacity of NMC622, Na + NMC622, NMC622 + Nd and Na + NMC622 + Nd drops to 93, 91, 93, and 82% of their initial values. With increasing C-rate (0.2, 0.5, and 1C), a further decrease in the capacity of all materials is observed, but the order of their electrochemical activity is always the same, i.e., NMC622 + Nd > NMC622 > Na + NMC622 > Na + NMC622 + Nd. The dual-doping with Na and Nd causes a strong deterioration of the electrochemical performance of the NMC622 material. A possible explanation may be the occupancy of 3a sites by dopant ions causing a hindered diffusion of Li
+
ions during charge–discharge processes. The cycling performance test (Fig. 1b) reveals a gradual capacity fading during long-term cycling of all the tested materials. In the case of the dual-doped Na + NMC622 + Nd material, the loss of capacity after 100 cycles at 0.5C was significant and higher than 50%. The undoped NMC622 retains 62.7% of its initial capacity, whereas doping with Na or Nd significantly improves the stability of the NMC622 material. The capacity retention for the Nd-doped and Na-doped materials is 76.4 and 80.5%, respectively. To rationalize the described electrochemical behaviour of the studied materials and explain the effect of dual-doping (with Na and Nd ions) on the NMC622 cathode properties, detailed characterization studies of the undoped and doped cathode powders, as well as the prepared electrodes, were performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Na and Nd doping
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2 shows XRD patterns of the studied cathode powders in the range of 10–70°. The doped samples exhibit diffraction peaks in the same positions as those of undoped NMC622, suggesting that doping had no apparent influence on the crystal structure of the studied cathodes. All the diffraction peaks are clear and sharp, indicating a well-crystallized structure and can be indexed to a well-defined layered α -NaFeO2 hexagonal structure (Hashem et al., 2011). Looking closely at the magnified regions of the (003) peak (Fig. S1b), it can be seen that for the Na-doped NMC622 cathodes, the 2 θ angle of the peak moves slightly to a lower angle, indicating the increment of the c-axis. This indicates that sodium ions successfully doped into the NMC622 lattice structure, subsequently enlarging the Li layer spacing (Xie et al., 2016). At the same time, from Fig. S1c,d, it can be found that the two pairs of (006)/(102) and (108)/(110) peaks at around 2
θ = 40 °
and 2
θ = 65 °
are distinctly separated for NMC622, Na + NMC622 and NMC622 + Nd, which indicates that they have a well-defined layered structure (Hashem et al., 2011). In the case of Na + NMC622 + Nd, these peaks are much less separated, reflecting poorer ordering of the layered structure (Hashem et al., 2011). Small amounts of impurities were found for NMC622 + Nd and Na + NMC622 + Nd. A few weak peaks, however, more pronounced for Na + NMC622 + Nd, were identified in the range of 20–35° corresponding to Nd-containing compounds (Fig. 2). This may indicate the incomplete incorporation of the Nd ions into the structure of the cathode powders.

The results of the Rietveld analysis are presented in Table 2. The determined value of the a-lattice parameter related to the metal–metal distance in MO2 layers, as expected, does not significantly change after Na doping into the interior of the crystal. This result agrees with previous studies of the Na-doped NMC cathodes (Ates et al., 2014; Hashem et al., 2022). The Nd-doping results in a decrease of the a-lattice parameter compared to undoped NMC622. This result could be expected since the incorporation of Nd
3 +
ions into the TM layer results in the formation of NdO bonds of high binding energy (Shi et al., 2020). The binding energy of NdO is 703 kJ mol −1, which is significantly higher than that of LiO (340.5 kJ mol −1), NiO (366 kJ mol −1), CoO (397.4 kJ mol −1), MnO (362 kJ mol −1) (Luo, 2007). It can be thus inferred that doping with Nd
3 +
can make the lattice structure more stable by forming strong NdO bonds in the TM layer. The effectiveness of Nd-doping is thus clearly observed in enhanced rate capability and cycling stability of the Nd-doped NMC622 (Fig. 1). It is also worth noting that the binding energy of NdO is much higher than that of AlO (501.9 kJ mol−1), and Al is commonly seen as a dopant in Ni-rich cathodes that bonds strongly with oxygen, therefore preventing the lattice collapse (Shi et al., 2020).

The interlayer distance, described by c-lattice parameter, for the Na-doped NMC622 cathodes is enlarged compared to undoped NMC622. This confirms the successful Na-ion doping (Xie et al., 2016). It can be thus stated that Li layer spacing can be enlarged after sodium treatment. For Nd-doped NMC622, the decrease in the value of cell parameter c is observed compared to undoped NMC622. It can be seen as an interesting result since, in other studies devoted to the rare-earth elements (REEs) doping of NMC cathodes, the increase of the c-lattice parameter was observed (Xu et al., 2019; Jia et al., 2017; Feng et al., 2021). The explanation is that the small amount of REE occupies the position of Ni
3 +
, and because the ionic radius of REE is larger than that of Ni
3 +
, REE dopants expand the Li-ion diffusion channel (Xu et al., 2019; Jia et al., 2017). However, the increment of the parameter c when introducing the REE or TM dopants into the TM layer was also observed (Shi et al., 2020; Eilers-Rethwisch et al., 2018). A detailed explanation for this can be drawn from the study of Eilers-Rethwisch et al. (2018). As reported, the change in the c-lattice parameter is not directly related to the ionic radius of the dopant but rather to the strength of the metal-oxygen bond. With the increase of metal-oxygen bond strength, the repulsion of the Li-interlayer is minimized. Due to higher binding energy, the bond lengths between metal and oxygen become shorter, and the electron density is shifted more towards the metal ions. The lower electron density on the oxygen leads to lower repulsion of the oxygen atoms, and thereby the c-lattice parameter becomes smaller. Thus, it can be stated that the lower c expansion in NMC622 + Nd is related to a higher binding energy of NdO.

For dual-doped NMC622, the increase in the lattice parameters a and c compared to undoped NMC622 is observed (Table 2). The increase in the value of the parameter c could be expected due to the incorporation of Na ions into the interior of the crystal (Xie et al., 2016). In contrast, the increase in the value of the a-lattice parameter as well as the cell volume (V), is unexpected. It indicates the structural disorder induced by the simultaneous introduction of Na and Nd dopants. It might be due to the poorer ordering of the layered structure of Na + NMC622 + Nd, as indicated in Fig. S1c,d. The c/a ratio is also an important parameter related to the integrity of the layered crystal structure and the stability of the cathode material (Mou et al., 2021). For all the studied materials c/a value is greater than 4.9. However, for the dual-doped Na + NMC622 + Nd cathode, the c/a parameter is the lowest, which also reflects its weaker ordering of the layered structure.

The surface composition of the cathode powders was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Fig. 3). The presence of oxygen, nickel, cobalt, manganese and lithium was confirmed for all samples. An XPS C 1s line of small intensity was also identified, indicating slight carbon contamination. The photoelectron signal originating from neodymium atoms present in the corresponding materials was not detected. XPS Na 1s line was observed only in the spectrum acquired for sample Na + NMC622. High-resolution spectra were acquired in the binding energy region between 45 and 85 eV. In this region, a Li 1s line and Ni 3p, Co 3p, and Mn 3p line can be observed (Fig. 3). Low-intensity XPS Li 1s line is observed at the binding energy of about 55 eV. There is a slight variance in the intensity of this line between samples. There is no significant variation in the relative intensity and envelope shape for XPS Ni 3p, XPS Co 3p and XPS Mn 3p lines. The addition of sodium or neodymium does not influence the chemical state of these three elements occurring in the examined materials. The positions 3p transitions from Ni, Co, and Mn correspond to the occurrence of the respective oxides of these elements.

High-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM-HAADF) was used to observe the microstructure and morphology of the samples. As shown in Figs. 4–8, the doped cathode powders do not show significant morphological differences in relation to the pristine NMC622. All powders are in the form of grains formed by agglomerated nanoparticles of irregular shapes and 0.5–
1
μ m
in size. The EDX mapping of Ni, Mn and Co shows a uniform distribution of these elements on the particle surface. For Na-doped NMC622 (Fig. 5), the uniform distribution of Na ions over the entire nanoparticle region is also observed. It can be thus inferred that Na ions doped into the interior of the crystal. For Nd-doped NMC622 (Fig. 6), the distribution of neodymium is not uniform. Both the phase-separated Nd-containing particles (Zybert et al., 2022), with the size of tens of nanometres (Fig. 6), as well as uniformly distributed Nd over the entire nanoparticle region (Fig. 7), can be observed. It suggests that Nd ions can, to some extent, replace the transition metal (TM) ions in the TM layer. In the case of Na, Nd dual-doped NMC622 (Fig. 8), a uniform distribution of Na ions and Nd precipitates as a secondary phase is detected. The above results are consistent with the results of XRD observations.

The structural and microscopic characterization of the cycled electrodes provided information about the structural degradation of the electrodes due to electrochemical reactions occurring during long-term cycling. Fig. S2 shows the ex situ XRD patterns of the discharged cathodes after cycling. It can be observed that the (108) peak of Na + NMC622 and NMC622 + Nd remained sharp after 100 cycles, but for NMC622 significantly diminished. For Na + NMC622 + Nd, the (108) peak practically collapsed. This demonstrates that Na-doping in Na + NMC622 and Nd-doping in NMC622 + Nd can stabilize the layered structure during cycling (Zhang et al., 2020). In contrast, the combination of two dopants, Na and Nd, in Na + NMC622 + Nd resulted in severe structural degradation of the cathode. These results agree with the cycling stability trend observed for the studied cathodes (Na + NMC622 > NMC622 + Nd > NMC622 > Na + NMC622 + Nd) (Fig. 1b).

Figs. S3–S6 present SEM and TEM images of the electrode before and after cycling. For the NMC622 pristine electrode (Fig. S3), repeated charge/discharge at high voltages did not significantly change the surface of the electrode. It looks almost intact after long-term cycling. For the Na + NMC622 electrode (Fig. S4), a thin amorphous film on the surface of the cycled electrode was formed. The same effect was detected in the case of the NMC622 + Nd electrode (Fig. S5). Moreover, slight agglomeration of particles also occurred. Degradation of the working electrode surface due to contact with electrolyte and electrochemical processes during charging/discharging seems to be the most pronounced for the dual-doped Na + NMC622 + Nd material (Fig. S6). The layer present on the electrode surface can cause sluggish transportation of charge and electrons. This, in turn, affects the cathode cycling behaviour, resulting in the gradual decrease of the capacity during long-term cycling, as is observed for Na + NMC622 + Nd (Fig. 1b).

Foreign-ion doping is one of the main problems in the design of Ni-rich cathode materials as a perspective electrode for Li-ion batteries. Due to their rapid performance degradation, it is impossible to fully use the opportunities resulting from the high nickel content, mainly high capacity (Zhang, 2020). Mitigating the unfavourable mechanisms of structural and chemical instability, such as surface side reactions, Ni/Li cation mixing, surface phase transition, oxygen evolution, and transition metal ion dissolution, may be realized by doping. The literature has already reported many examples of doping and its influence on the electrochemical performance of cathode materials (Cui et al., 2023). The dopants are usually divided into two categories according to their position in the layered structure. Doping ions placed in the positions normally occupied by nickel ions in MO2 layers most often inhibit cation mixing, change particle morphology, form in-situ coating layers, or inhibit the generation of microcracks. By strong bonding with the oxygen, they help to prevent the irreversible release of lattice O and improve the safety performance of LIBs. Doping ions in the positions normally occupied by Li
+
ions in the interlayered spaces expand the Li
+
ions pathway enhancing diffusion kinetic. They also decrease cation mixing and elevate the migration barrier of TM ions, hindering the unfavourable phase transitions from layered structure to spinel or disordered rock-salt (Cui et al., 2023; Zybert et al., 2022). “Pillar ions” stabilize the layered structure and prevent its collapse during Li
+
intercalation/deintercalation. In this work, we attempted to simultaneously use these two types of dopants. The NMC622 cathode material dual-doped with sodium and neodymium was designed to study if Na
+
and Nd
3 +
ions, i.e., dopants located in different positions in the layered structure and playing various roles, can synergistically improve the electrochemical performance of the cathode materials in Li-ion battery. The combination of detailed characterization studies provides a consistent interpretation, with the conclusions that:

ce:list-item.ce:para:
The Na ions were successfully doped into the interior of the layered LiNi
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
O2 cathode. It was confirmed by the slight shift of the peak (003) reflection in XRD towards​ a lower angle region, indicating that the c-axis increased upon the sodium treatment (Fig. 1, Fig. S1, and Table 2). Further, as revealed in the microscopic observations (Fig. 5), the uniform distribution of Na over the entire nanoparticle region was visible. The positive effect of Na doping on the electrochemical performance was clearly noted. Despite the discharge capacity of Na + NMC622 (145.9 mAh g−1) at 0.1C was almost the same as that of undoped NMC622 (146.6 mAh g−1), Na-doped NMC622 displayed significantly improved capacity retention as compared to that of undoped NMC622 (80.5% vs. 62.7%) (Fig. 1b). The Li-ion diffusivity was also slightly improved as a result of sodium doping (Table 1). It is therefore confirmed that the doping of Na ions into the interior of the crystal expands the Li interslab spacing, which contributes to the favourable Li
+
diffusion kinetics. These findings align with observations reported by Xie et al. (2016), revealing that sodium dopant positively affects the NCA cathode electrochemical behaviour by improving the Li-ion diffusion kinetics.

The Nd ions were at least partially incorporated into the transition-metal (TM) layer of the LiNi
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
O2 cathode. It was verified by the shortened metal–metal distance in the TM layer, as revealed by the decrease of the a-lattice parameter upon Nd dopant introduction (Table 2). This is because neodymium forms stronger bonds with oxygen than nickel, manganese and cobalt (Shi et al., 2020; Luo, 2007). As a consequence, a decrease in the a-lattice parameter was observed. At the same time, as revealed in microscopic observations (Figs. 6 and 7), both the phase-separated Nd-containing particles and uniformly distributed Nd over the entire nanoparticle region were visible. It can be thus stated that Nd was partially doped into the bulk of LiNi
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
O2. At the same time, the excessive Nd existed as the phase-separated particles. The positive effect of doping with Nd on the electrochemical behaviour was clearly observed. Apart from the higher discharge capacity of NMC622 + Nd at 0.1C (154.4 mAh g−1) than that of undoped NMC622 (146.6 mAh g−1), the Nd-doped NMC622 cathode also showed improved capacity retention and Li-ion diffusivity as compared to that of undoped NMC622 (Fig. 1b and Table 1). The presence of neodymium, as a dopant in the LiNi
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
O2 cathode, stabilizes the layered structure during cycling. Neodymium forms bonds with oxygen stronger than transition metals, which mitigates the lattice collapse. These findings support the similar conclusion reached by Shi et al. (2020), who showed that the enhanced structural stability of the NMC cathode could be achieved by doping on Ni sites with Nd, which forms stronger bonds with oxygen than Ni does.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (3a)
ce:list-item.ce:para:
The combination of Na and Nd as the dopants resulted in much poorer electrochemical and cycling performance of the dual-doped LiNi
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
O2 cathode (Fig. 1). It is an intriguing result since, as mentioned above, the Na and Nd dopants themselves considerably improved the electrochemical performance of NMC622 (Fig. 1). Therefore, it was reasonable to expect that the dual-doped Na + NMC622 + Nd cathode, in which Na and Nd play different but complementary roles, would result in improved electrochemical performance compared to that of the single-doped and undoped NMC622 cathodes. It is not clear what caused the poor performance of dual-doped NMC622. The experimental data suggest that the simultaneous doping of Na and Nd caused the deterioration of the crystal structure. The dual-doped cathode did not show a well-developed layered structure as it was for single-doped cathodes (Figs. 2 and S1). It is presumed to be a consequence of excessive amounts of dopants and, as a result, introduced impurities, which are not desirable since they affect the physical properties of inorganic crystals (Vlaskin et al., 2013).

ce:list-item.ce:label: (3b)
ce:list-item.ce:para:
This study lacks the extent of dopant amounts as well as the dopants ratio. As mentioned earlier, the optimal doping concentrations of Na and Nd were selected based on previous works (Zybert et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2016; He et al., 2019). On this basis, the Na, Nd dual-doped LiNi
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
O2 cathode was prepared. It seemed reasonable since the Na and Nd dopant amounts were optimized for single-doped cathodes. Be that as it may, the study of one concentration of Na and Nd dopants can be perceived as the drawback of the current methodology since the properties of multi-doped cathodes can be finely tuned by varying the dopant amounts and dopants ratio (Ghorbanzadeh et al., 2018). Nonetheless, one interesting conclusion that can be drawn from this work is that the optimal dopant amount for a single-doped cathode does not have to be the optimal amount for a dual-doped cathode. The idea of using sodium and neodymium as the dopants is still believed to be promising since Na and Nd can play different but complementary roles. Therefore, we anticipate this study to be the starting point for further investigations dedicated to determining the optimal Na and Nd dopant concentrations as well as Na/Nd ratio in the LiNi
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
O2 cathode.

ce:section.ce:para: It is clearly seen that the effect of single-element doping is limited and often improves only selected properties (e.g., doping with sodium improved electrode stability but slightly lowered its discharge capacity). It should also be remembered that introducing a certain amount of electrochemically inactive ions into the material structure will cause capacity deterioration. Therefore, when modifying Ni-rich cathode materials, a compromise should be found between the expected capacity, energy density and cycle stability. Hence, a dual-doping or multi-element doping strategy with a synergistic modification effect seems promising and perspective. However, its implementation is very challenging. It requires a comprehensive understanding and development of many important aspects, including (i) selection of doping elements, (ii) optimization of doping concentrations, (iii) ensuring homogeneous distribution of dopants, and (iv) control of doping sites. Adopting multiple convenient doping methods or even designing novel procedures to effectively introduce multiple dopants may also be necessary.

In summary, the LiNi
0 . 6
Mn
0 . 2
Co
0 . 2
O2 materials doped with sodium and/or neodymium were synthesized and used as the cathodes for Li-ion batteries. The strategies of single- and dual-element doping were applied to study the effect of Na and Nd doping on the electrochemical behaviour of the modified NMC622 materials. The Na-doped NMC622 cathode exhibited improved capacity retention of 80.5% after 100 cycles, which is superior to the undoped NMC622 (62.7%), possibly owing to enlarged Li layer spacing and decreased Li-ion migration activation energy. For the Nd-doped NMC622 cathode, both the initial discharge capacity and capacity retention were greatly improved compared to the undoped one. This can be related to the enhanced structural stability brought by the formation of strong bonds between neodymium and oxygen atoms. In contrast, the dual-doping of Na and Nd in NMC622 resulted in its much poorer electrochemical and cycling performance. It is not clear what caused the poor performance of the dual-doped NMC622 material. The experimental data suggest that the combination of Na and Nd dopants caused the deterioration of crystal structure, possibly due to the introduced impurities. This consequently affected the structural stability of the dual-doped material, leading to the lowest discharge capacity and capacity retention among the studied NMC622 materials. Nevertheless, the dual-doping strategy with synergistic modification effect still seems to be promising and perspective. However, its implementation is undeniably challenging and requires extensive future study to understand the multi-doping effect in NMC cathode materials.

Andrzej Ostrowski: Carried out the XRD measurements. Kamil Sobczak: Conducted the SEM, TEM, and HAADF-STEM characterizations. Dariusz Moszyński: Carried out the XPS measurements. Wioletta Raróg-Pilecka: Provided suggestions and feedback on the study. Bartosz Hamankiewicz: Performed the electrochemical measurements.
